title: Why Engineering and Architecture Practices Finish June Busy and Start August Tight on Cash
linkedin:
Engineering consultancies are running through their strongest billing month of the year.
The August bank account rarely matches.

For most engineering and architecture practices, June is peak billing time. Government clients clearing remaining capital budgets before June 30 drive a real surge in project activations and new instructions. The fee register fills and monthly revenue records are set.

What doesn't get modelled is what happens between June 30 and mid-August.

June hours billed in early July on 30-day government client terms settle in mid-August. The WIP balance at June 30 is work already done and not yet converted to cash. Meanwhile July brings the first payroll at new award rates from 1 July, the Q4 superannuation guarantee due on 28 July, and a full month of overhead before most June revenue has cleared.

The gap is predictable. It just rarely gets planned for before it arrives.

There's a second thing worth examining at EOFY: the full-year billable ratio.

For most consultancies, twelve months of timesheet data makes this visible properly for the first time. The practice-wide average is rarely the most useful number. What the data reveals is the spread: which fee earners run consistently above average, which project types produce the best utilisation, and whether the June government surge is masking a lower average through the rest of the year.

Both analyses are available in the accounting and timesheet data sitting in most practices right now. The engineering and architecture principals who run this work before the year closes start August with a clear picture rather than a quiet surprise.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

June is consistently the strongest billing month for engineering and architecture practices in Queensland and NSW. Government clients clearing capital budgets before June 30 create a real surge in project activity.

But here's what's easy to miss: most of that June work goes out as a July invoice on 30-day terms. The cash lands in August. Meanwhile, July runs at new award rates from day one and the Q4 superannuation guarantee falls due on 28 July.

The practices that model this in June are in a very different position than those that discover it in their August bank statement.

What does your July cash picture actually look like this year?

#AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
google:
If you run an engineering or architecture practice in Queensland, NSW, or Victoria, June is likely the busiest billing month of the year. Government clients clearing remaining capital budgets before June 30 drive a surge of project activations and new instructions that rarely appears at any other time in the calendar.

What is worth planning for is what comes after.

Most June work is invoiced in early July on 30-day terms. That means the cash arrives in mid-August. July itself brings new award rate costs from the first payroll of the new financial year, the Q4 superannuation guarantee due on 28 July for the April to June quarter, and a full month of operational costs before most June revenue has cleared the collection cycle.

The other exercise worth completing before the financial year closes is the full-year billable ratio. Twelve months of timesheet data show what share of fee earner time was actually charged to client projects and which project types produced the best utilisation. For most practices, this picture differs from what the revenue line suggests.

Both analyses are sitting in the accounting and timesheet systems most practices already use.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
